---
title: "Model Interpretation"
code-tools: false
description: "Interpreting machine learning models using software development best practices to ensure reproducibility and scalability of data science workflows."
date: 2026-02-01
categories: [Python, Scikit-learn, Model Evaluation, Hyperparameter Tuning, Package Development]
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    theme: lux
---


### The value of this group project to me

- **Technical skills**:
    - creating reusable functions and scripts for data preprocessing, modeling, and evaluation to promote reproducibility and scalability.
    - exploring new platform Netlify for hosting and sharing data science projects.
    - applying data cleaning, EDA, machine learning models (baseline, KNN, SVM RBF), and visualization techniques. 

- **Collaboration**: 
    - working with a team of 3 data science students to divide tasks, share insights, and integrate work into a cohesive project.

- **Project management**: 
    - coordinating timelines, version control, and communication to ensure smooth progress.
    - pushing code to GitHub and deploying the final report on Netlify for public access.

---

### Overview

**`model_auto_interpret`** is a Python package that streamlines the repetitive parts of building and comparing scikit-learn classification models — hyperparameter tuning, cross-model comparison, and performance evaluation. Rather than rewriting the same boilerplate for every project, the package wraps these steps into three reusable functions, sitting in the gap between scikit-learn's low-level utilities and visualization-only libraries like `mlxtend`. The package was built as part of **UBC Master of Data Science, DSCI 524 Collaborative Software Development course**, with a team of 3: Daisy (Ying) Zhou, Yasaman Baher, and myself.

### Project repository

**GitHub repo:** [UBC-MDS/model_interpretation](https://github.com/canadasung/model_interpretation){target="_blank"} (forked from school repo because it is not publicly accessible)

### Documentation & tutorial

**Docs site:** [machine-learning-model-interpretation.netlify.app](https://machine-learning-model-interpretation.netlify.app/){target="_blank"} — deployed on Netlify, including a full [tutorial](https://machine-learning-model-interpretation.netlify.app/tutorial){target="_blank"} and [API reference](https://machine-learning-model-interpretation.netlify.app/reference/){target="_blank"}.

---

### Installation

```bash
pip install -i https://test.pypi.org/simple/ model-auto-interpret
```

---

### Key Functions

The package centers on three functions covering the typical model-development workflow:

- **[`param_tuning_summary`](https://machine-learning-model-interpretation.netlify.app/reference/hyperparameter_tuning_summary.param_tuning_summary){target="_blank"}**: takes a fitted `GridSearchCV`/`RandomizedSearchCV` object and returns a clean summary dataframe of the best parameters and scores, plus the best estimator ready to use.

- **[`model_cv_metric_compare`](https://machine-learning-model-interpretation.netlify.app/reference/model_compare.model_cv_metric_compare){target="_blank"}**: evaluates multiple unfitted models side-by-side via cross-validation, returning accuracy, precision, recall, F1, and ROC-AUC in a single comparison table.

- **[`model_evaluation_plotting`](https://machine-learning-model-interpretation.netlify.app/reference/model_evaluation.model_evaluation_plotting){target="_blank"}**: scores a fitted model on held-out test data, returning accuracy/F2 metrics, a confusion matrix table, and a ready-to-plot confusion matrix display.

---

### Tutorial walkthrough

The package tutorial demonstrates the full workflow on the **Breast Cancer dataset** (455 training samples, 30 features, binary classification):

1. **Compare models**: `model_cv_metric_compare()` runs 5-fold cross-validation across Logistic Regression, SVC variants, Random Forest, Decision Tree, and Gradient Boosting, producing a single comparison table.
2. **Tune hyperparameters**: `param_tuning_summary()` extracts the best parameter configuration from `GridSearchCV`/`RandomizedSearchCV` results for the top candidate models.
3. **Evaluate on test data**: `model_evaluation_plotting()` scores the tuned model on the test set and generates a confusion matrix visualization.

**Result**: Random Forest emerged as the top performer at ~96% cross-validation accuracy. The tutorial closes with an end-to-end example on the Iris dataset, reaching 100% test accuracy.

---

### Key takeaways

- **Less boilerplate, more reproducibility**: collapsing tuning, comparison, and evaluation into three function calls makes model-development notebooks shorter and easier for teammates to rerun.
- **Designed for collaboration**: built with packaging best practices (tests, CI, semantic versioning, hosted docs) as the core learning objective of the course, not just the modeling logic itself.
